464da9fa87b033fbc74a.text:
**Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member onboarding & first 30 days**: no documented sequence for intake → assessment → goal-setting → first class experience → check-in cadence. This is your highest-churn window.
- **Churn & win-back**: no trigger-based process when members pause, cancel, or go dark. No re-engagement playbook.
- **Coach development & quality assurance**: no coaching standards audit, peer feedback loop, or progression framework. Class quality drifts without this.
- **Member data, privacy & GDPR**: no documented process for consent, data storage, retention, member requests, or incident response.
- **Referral programme mechanics**: no SOP on tracking, reward fulfilment, or ambassador management—leaves money on table.
- **Supply chain & ordering**: no process for inventory forecasting, vendor management, or reorder points (protein, towels, equipment wear items).
- **Complaints & incidents**: no escalation path, documentation standard, or resolution timeline. Health & safety gaps here too.
- **Milestone & outcomes tracking**: no SOP connecting member goals → progress measurement → celebration (drives retention & word-of-mouth).

**Needs updating**

- **Coach SOPs (20/24)**: likely missing weekly task prioritization, class planning templates, and how coaches document member progress/feedback in real time.
- **Client SOPs (23/33)**: probably gaps in member check-in frequency triggers, how to handle no-shows, and escalation when engagement drops.
- **Marketing (5/5)**: only 5 SOPs for marketing—almost certainly missing member communication calendar, email/SMS sequences, social content approval, and campaign performance review cadence.
- **Facilities (5/6)**: missing deep clean schedules, equipment maintenance logs, and emergency response protocols.

**Build next**

1. **

ef78fa4eea1cbb63abd4.text:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO RECOMMENDATIONS

## INCREASE REVENUE

• **Pricing increase overdue.** ARR/M has collapsed from £170 (Dec) to £120 (Jun): members are paying less or you're heavy on introductory/off-peak rates. With 147 active clients (your highest), you should be hitting £25k–£27k monthly revenue. Audit membership tiers now: raise standard rate by 10–15% (£12–£15/month) and migrate existing members on renewal. Target: £24k revenue by August.

• **Churn spike is killing growth.** June's 0% churn was a blip; May–Jun average is 2.87%, but Q2 (Mar–May) averaged 4.09% vs Q1 (Jan–Mar) at 5.64%. *Focus on retention first:* implement 30-day re-engagement drip (email, SMS, class feedback), add a 12-month loyalty discount (5% off), and conduct exit interviews. Stabilize churn below 2% to protect the 147-member base.

• **Upsell personal training & retail.** Revenue is stuck at £15k–£20k despite 147 members (avg spend £115–£137). Cross-sell PT packages (£30–£50/session, 2–4 per month per member = £300k annual potential if 10% uptake). Add branded water bottles, gym wear, protein powder (10–15% margin). Target: +£2k revenue/month from PT + retail by Q3.

## REDUCE EXPENSES

• **Non-people costs are the culprit.** May (£9.2k) → June (£9.4k): flat-lining despite revenue drop to £17.7k (down £2.5k). Jan–Mar was worse (£8–£16k). Breakdown required: utilities (higher post-relocation?), supplies, software, rent. If rent increased post-move, that's locked-in; but variable costs (supplies, cleaning, laundry) should scale down in a slower month. Demand vendor quotes and renegotiate contracts before Q3 (typical renewal window).

• **Staffing model needs review.** Q2 payroll (Mar–May): £3,116–£3,550/month for 106–122 members = £29–£34 cost per member. November hit £25/member at 107 clients, so target is achievable. With 147 members, payroll should stay at £3,300–£3,400 (one PT + reception

f527670fd10b0672288e.text:
• **Redistribute Grace's classes immediately**: JoJo should take her Build: Lower sessions (17 per month) — this complements JoJo's existing Upper and Full Body strength work. Eilis takes Elevate (6) and Strongevity (3) to build her confidence with varied class types before she's ready for higher-demand slots.

• **Shift Grace's PT to Eilis**: Move the 9 Body Composition Scans (not real coaching) to any coach, and assign Grace's PT demand to Eilis (currently carrying only 11 PT vs JoJo's 44). This balances 1:1 load and develops Eilis's PT skills faster.

• **Reduce JoJo's Body Composition Scans**: JoJo is doing 20 scans per month — hand these to Eilis and Grace (non-delivery scans don't need a head coach). Frees ~4–5 hours of JoJo's time per month for higher-value work.

• **Create a Tuesday pairing for resilience**: JoJo owns Tuesdays alone, creating a bottleneck. Schedule Eilis to shadow or co-deliver one Tuesday class per month (no hard constraint prevents this), so there's a backup and knowledge transfer happens before someone gets ill.

• **No Sweat Intros (3 sessions) should rotate**: Grace is the only one delivering these; assign them to Eilis or JoJo on a rota so no single coach carries a unique responsibility.

c17ea6e513949b5bd341.text:
**For quiet studio time:**

1. **Add a 30-min "Strength Basics" open-gym slot on Monday/Thursday mornings** (your quietest days) where members can drop in for coached barbell work or machines — bridges the gap for people intimidated by full Build classes and can convert them into regulars.

2. **Run a paid 4-week "Engine Fundamentals" workshop on Saturday mornings** (lowest attendance day) in a 45-min format with 6-8 members max — Engine is severely underattended, so repackage it as a structured intro course rather than a standalone class.

**For quieter classes themselves:**

3. **Move "Games Night" to Friday evening and pair it with a social post targeting members who attended that week's other classes** — it's your lowest-attended class (14 in 30 days) and likely buried in a bad slot; Friday's your third-busiest day, so better foot traffic + direct invites to recent attendees could triple sign-ups.

47b0548900970eee6400.text:
# Service Menu Execution for The Forge

• **Start with your margin reality TODAY** — Pull last month's revenue by service type (classes, PT, memberships, retail, whatever you offer). What's actually profitable? Build your menu around what makes money, not what sounds good. Grace, if a service barely covers costs, don't list it.

• **Create TWO separate docs immediately** — One visual menu for members (simple, benefit-focused pricing, what's in each tier), one operational sheet for coaches (inclusions checklist, upsell triggers, boundaries). Coaches need to know exactly when a member gets X vs. Y.

• **Price with anchor points** — Don't just list costs. Use tiering: entry-level membership, mid-tier (membership + monthly PT sessions), premium (all-in). Make the middle option 60% of the total package price — that's where most conversions happen.

• **Embed this into your sales flow NOW** — Don't just create a document and file it. Train your front desk/intake team TODAY on pitch order: membership first, then contextually offer PT/packages. Test it with 5 new sign-ups this week.

• **QR codes to digital pricing** — Print menus, yes—but link to a living online version you can edit without reprinting. Services and prices will change; make it easy to update without waste.

**Do the margin audit first.** Everything else flows from that.

e4aeda2aa0d1508a8f79.text:
**Insights**

Your cost-per-lead has collapsed to £3—down from £21 in June—but you're converting at 8%, a massive drop from April's 29%. You've bought volume cheap, but you're getting the wrong people. The profit is still solid (£1,082) because spend is low, but you're on a treadmill: you need 13 sales to match June's revenue, yet July only closed 7 despite 4.5x more leads. This suggests the creative, targeting, or landing experience changed in July—or you've widened the net so far you're catching people with no genuine intent.

Your April performance (£6/lead, 29% close) is the benchmark to chase; that's when cost-per-acquisition was ~£21 and you were profitable at scale. May and June show cost creeping up (targeting fatigue + seasonality, likely), but June still converted 65%—meaning the *quality* held. July broke the pattern: cheap leads, weak conversion. Something shifted.

**Actions**

1. **Audit your July targeting & creative immediately.** Pull the exact audience segments, placements, and ad creative that drove those 90 leads. Compare to April. You've likely either broadened age/location too wide, dropped to Broad/Lookalike, or switched creative to something that generates curiosity but not intent (e.g. "Transform your body in 6 weeks" vs "Women-only gym in Belfast—try free"). Test reverting to April's exact targeting + top-performing April creative, ring-fence a £50 budget, and measure quality (close rate) not volume.

2. **Implement a lead-quality score before you scale.** You're flying blind on whether the 90 leads are cold browsers or hot prospects. Add a qualifying question at lead capture: "Have you been to a gym in the past 12 months? Yes/No" or "When do you want to start? This week / next month / considering." Segment by response and only nurture/follow-up the warm cohort first. This will reveal if July's problem is lead quality or sales process.

3. **Run a simple A/B next month: volume vs. quality.** Split budget 50/

fbb52f88c48e54607ffa.text:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO REPORT

## REVENUE — SINGLE BIGGEST PRIORITY

• **Rev/member collapsing: £138 (April) → £110 (June). Target £130+ immediately.** Churn spiked to 12.7% in May post-relocation; now normalised at 3.3%, but June revenue £15,765 is 6.4% down on May despite adding 5 net members (138 vs 143). You're signing people but not monetising them — either new joiners on intro rates or existing members downgrading. Audit membership tier mix and bundle strength now. Action: run a 48-hour price-hold flash sale (£135/month → £145/month for new sign-ups) to test elasticity; if uptake stays ≥5 per month, lock it in.

• **Quiet-hours upsell gap.** Peak revenue months (Feb £15,502, April £16,857) sit at 119–122 active members; June is 143 members but £15,765 revenue. You have 21 extra bodies not generating proportional income. Introduce drop-in class packs (10 × £15 = £150 per non-member guest), corporate day passes (£12/session for local offices), and 1:1 PT at £45/session. Target: capture £500/month from non-member footfall.

• **Membership retention cliff at 12.7% (May) requires root-cause fix.** Post-relocation churn spike suggests new studio friction (timetable changes, commute, facility teething). Exit surveys via email to May leavers immediately. If >40% cite "location," prioritise shuttle pass or satellite studio. If facility-based, assign a free month credit to at-risk cohorts (members with <6 visits/month).

---

## EXPENSES — FOCUS ON MARCH & JUNE SPIKES

• **Cash out spiked £23,328 (March) and £20,644 (June) with no revenue justification.** March was pre-relocation build (acceptable); June is post-move but still 26% above your May/Feb baseline (£16,510–£16,639). Call your landlord and utilities supplier: new premises likely under-contracted. Demand cost review on rates, heating, waste. April spend at £39,015 was relocation capex — exclude it — but June at £20,644 against £15,765 revenue means your **operating margin on trades is now negative** (assuming 60% typical COGS on gym). You're bleeding cash. Action
